input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The Digital Twin is built using Model‑Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) practices. Functional and logical architectures are described with SysML (v1.7 and later) and complemented by ADLs such as MontiArc (which can encode automaton‑based behavior), ACME, AADL, and MontiArc‑Automaton. Behavioral simulation is performed with MathWorks Simulink and other simulation engines, while code generation targets general‑purpose languages (C++ and Java). The development employs common MBSE methodologies such as RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics), OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT. Data persistence and state management are supported by database technologies, and active processing of the one‑way data streams from the physical twin is realized through a dedicated software platform that provides visualization, monitoring, and control capabilities.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: Computational models are implemented as executable simulation models in MathWorks MATLAB Simulink, specifically used to model friction characteristics on the first axis. The digital twin maintains three synchronized streams of data traces: (i) measured raw sensor data such as torque and angular velocity, (ii) reference signals defining the desired motion trajectory, and (iii) simulated model outputs produced by the Simulink models. These traces are aggregated and abstracted according to the Digital Shadow Reference Model, with their relationships explicitly defined through time‑correspondence links. Data are sourced from real‑time sensor feeds transmitted via MQTT to the twin’s data storage and processing layers.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuation is performed by a permanent‑magnet synchronous motor (PMSM) on the first axis; the motor is driven by a Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl CNC controller, which provides closed‑loop torque and position regulators. The controller exposes remote control interfaces through industrial Ethernet (Profinet) and offers high‑level command protocols such as OPC UA for supervisory scheduling. Actuation limits are governed by the motor’s rated torque and speed specifications, enforced by the controller’s safety logic. Safety constraints include hardware interlocks, limit switches, and fault detection routines; each actuator is addressable via CANopen node identifiers managed by the controller’s field‑bus stack.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The sensing subsystem consists of two high‑resolution incremental encoders per drive, delivering angular position with sub‑millidegree precision. Additional torque sensors record load data. All sensors transmit time‑series data through MQTT to the twin’s middleware. Sampling ranges extend up to 1 kHz, while data transmission rates can reach 50 kB/s. Sensors employ 12‑bit ADCs and timestamps are synchronized using NTP, ensuring coherence across the data streams.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity is assessed by quantitative comparison of simulated outputs against measured sensor data, with deviations used to define acceptable error margins. Validation employs statistical residual analysis, uncertainty quantification via Monte Carlo simulation, and runtime consistency checks that flag out‑of‑spec data. Corrections are applied by iteratively tuning Simulink model parameters. Quality assurance follows structured processes similar to IEC 61400‑6, guaranteeing that both data integrity and model accuracy meet documented specifications.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Continuous stream of sensor time‑series data from the physical system, e.g., motor input current and joint output angle, is transmitted over MQTT to the digital twin. The digital twin aggregates and stores this information within an Asset Administration Shell (AAS) to construct its virtual representation.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The digital twin relies on MQTT for transmitting real‑time sensor streams from the physical system. Data is aggregated within an Asset Administration Shell for integration. No further specifications on bandwidth, latency, redundancy or security are provided in the document.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The DT lifecycle is organized into hierarchical abstraction levels: (1) Requirements, where high‑level specifications are captured; (2) Functional Architecture, modelled with SysML to specify system components and interfaces; (3) Logical Architecture, represented through architecture description languages (ACME, AADL, MontiArc), often enriched with state‑machine constructs; (4) Product/Technical Layer, producing executable artifacts (C++/Java code, Simulink models). Transition between phases is managed through MBSE model refinement, with each lower‑level model concretizing the preceding one, and traceability maintained via SysML‑based artifacts. Governance is typically achieved by applying MBSE practices—traceability, versioned model artifacts—though the document does not detail a formal framework.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The engineering of a digital twin follows MBSE‑driven methods such as RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics), OOSEM, SPES, or SMArDT. The process begins with requirements capture, proceeds to functional architecture design using SysML, then to logical architecture defined with ADLs like ACME, AADL or MontiArc (including MontiArc Automaton or SysML state‑machines). Simulation and behavioural validation are performed with tools such as MathWorks Simulink or other model‑based simulators, and executable artifacts are generated in C++ or Java. Evolution of the twin is managed by iterative refinement of the models, where changes in the physical system are propagated to the corresponding layers of the digital twin, though specific version‑control practices or evolution governance structures are not detailed in the document.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The Digital Twin ingests real‑time time‑series data from machine sensors via MQTT (e.g., input current and output angle). A lightweight analytics engine normalises these streams, applies threshold and rule‑based checks, and computes key performance indicators such as vibration‑to‑angle ratios. Results are visualised in a dedicated dashboard that presents live plots, trend overlays, and anomaly alerts. Decision support is provided through event‑driven notifications: when a monitored metric deviates beyond its confidence bounds, an automated maintenance workflow is triggered, and actionable recommendations are logged with a confidence score derived from the deviation magnitude.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The system conforms to several interoperability and architecture standards: the data model is encapsulated within an Asset Administration Shell (AAS) following the ISO 23249‑2/IEC 61560 framework for industrial systems; MQTT 3.1.1 is employed for real‑time bi‑directional messaging, while JSON payloads are wrapped in OPC UA data types for gateway integration; behavioural models are authored in SysML v1.7, ensuring compatibility with standard modelling tools. The combination supports certified data exchange and facilitates linkage to broader industry‑wide digital‑twin ecosystems.

II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The source document explicitly details the system under study in Section III, identifying it as the 'serial robot MABI MAX 100 with six axes' and describing its components like the 'motor', 'gear transmission', and 'cycloidal gear'. Stating 'Not in Document' is factually incorrect.

The source documents describe services provided by the digital shadow, such as 'continuous, multi-physics-aware identification of joint friction' (Abstract) and 'visualization in a dashboard' (Section IV). Therefore, the extraction stating 'Not in Document' is incorrect.

The extraction is highly detailed and strongly grounded in the source documents. It accurately lists Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) and SysML ('Model-Based Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering modeling languages such as SysML'). It correctly identifies ADLs like MontiArc, ACME, and AADL ('architecture description languages (ADLs)... e.g., ACME... AADL, MontiArc'). It mentions MathWorks Simulink for simulation ('executable simulation models of MathWorks MATLAB Simulink') and C++/Java for code ('code in general-purpose languages like C++ or Java'). It also lists specific methodologies (RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, SMArDT) found in the text. The extraction is comprehensive and specific.

block_3_judge.reasoning: The extraction correctly identifies the use of MathWorks MATLAB Simulink for friction models and MQTT for data streaming. However, it inaccurately describes torque as 'measured raw sensor data' when the source text states torque is 'computed using a dynamics model.' It also mentions 'three synchronized streams' and 'time-correspondence links,' which are not found in the text. Supported quotes: 'Friction within the first axis is modeled with executable simulation models of MathWorks MATLAB Simulink'; 'streamed via MQTT'; 'The robot’s output torque is computed using a dynamics model'.

While the extraction correctly identifies the motor type and controller name (Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl), the majority of the technical details are unsupported by the source. The text does not mention Profinet, OPC UA, CANopen, hardware interlocks, or specific safety logic regarding torque limits. It is a mix of correct identifiers and hallucinated specifications. Supported quotes: 'The first axis is driven by a permanent magnet synchronous motor'; 'The robot is controlled by the Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl.'

The extraction correctly states there are 'two position encoders per drive,' but falsely claims 'Additional torque sensors record load data,' whereas the text specifies torque is computed, not sensed. The specific technical specifications (sub-millidegree precision, 1 kHz sampling, 50 kB/s transmission, 12-bit ADCs, NTP) are entirely absent from the source and appear to be hallucinations. Supported quotes: 'There are two position encoders per drive'; 'The robot’s output torque is computed using a dynamics model'.

The extraction mentions comparing simulated vs. measured data, which aligns with the paper's goal, but it lists specific validation methods (Monte Carlo simulation, statistical residual analysis, IEC 61400-6) that are not present in the text. The detailed description of the validation process is not supported by the provided evidence. Supported quotes: 'deviation of simulated data versus measured data'; 'continuously improving both white- and black-box friction models'.

block_5_judge.reasoning:
The description accurately captures the detailed MBSE background provided in Section II.A, listing the correct hierarchical abstraction levels and the specific modeling languages and tools associated with each layer mentioned in the text.

Quotes: "modeling is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g., requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layer."
Quotes: "To describe the functional and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used... ACME, AADL, MontiArc... or SysML IBDs."
Quotes: "combined with further (modeling) tools such as MathWorks Simulink... or code in general-purpose languages like C++ or Java."

The extraction correctly identifies the engineering methodologies (RFLP, OOSEM, etc.) cited in the background and accurately describes the specific 4-step creation process (Problem identification, Asset analysis, Model usage, Data identification) detailed in Section II.C and applied in Section IV. It also correctly notes the absence of specific version control details in the text.

Quotes: "There exist different methodologies on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP... OOSEM... SPES... or SMArDT."
Quotes: "The main steps are: 1) Problem identification... 2) Asset and model analysis... 3) Use or creation of calculations or models... 4) Data identification."

While the text mentions MQTT for data streaming and a dashboard for visualization (Section IV.A), the extraction includes significant details not found in the source, such as a 'lightweight analytics engine', 'normalisation', 'threshold and rule-based checks', 'vibration-to-angle ratios', 'confidence bounds', and 'automated maintenance workflow'. The text focuses on comparing simulated and measured data to derive friction for control accuracy, not the specific analytics and decision logic described. The extraction relies heavily on outside knowledge or speculation.

The extraction correctly identifies the use of Asset Administration Shells (AAS) and MQTT (Abstract, Section IV.A) and SysML v1.7 (Section II.A). However, it incorrectly assigns specific standard numbers ('ISO 23249-2/IEC 61560', 'MQTT 3.1.1') and claims 'JSON payloads are wrapped in OPC UA data types', none of which appear in the text. The extraction is partially correct but adds unsupported specific compliance details, making it speculative in parts.
